meta.title: Mercedes-Benz M270/M271 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost

hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz M270/M271 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The Mercedes M270 and M271 are compact turbocharged four-cylinder petrols — the M271's timing chain is a known weak point, while the M270 is generally more robust. Backed by real UK enquiries [ESTIMATED].

verdictRating.confidence: 85% confidence — based on owner reports and specialist workshop feedback across both engine families.

rows.score: 20 (Parts widely available; common across many Mercedes models)
8 (Recon cost ~£2,000 against lowest host value ~£3,000 = 67% (60–74% band → 8))

verdictRating.oneLineVerdict: "The M270/M271 are fine daily‑driver petrol turbos — but the M271's timing chain can fail catastrophically without warning — avoid any M271 without recent chain work."
verdictRating.bestFor: Buyers seeking affordable Mercedes petrol power with good parts support and simple maintenance.
verdictRating.avoidIf: Considering a high‑mileage M271 without documented timing‑chain history.

costGuide.labourEstimate: Fitting an M270/M271 typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [ESTIMATED].

cta.label: → Compare M270/M271 engine prices from UK specialists

items.question: Cost — "How much does an M270/M271 engine replacement cost?"
items.answer: A reconditioned M270/M271 typically costs £1,500–£2,500 supply only [ESTIMATED], varying by variant (M271 1.8L vs M270 2.0L) and specification. Fitting adds £600–£1,200 depending on labour rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

items.question: Reliability — "Is the M270/M271 a reliable engine?"
items.answer: The M270 is generally reliable with proper maintenance, while the M271 has a well‑documented timing‑chain tensioner weakness that can cause catastrophic failure if ignored. Both respond well to regular oil changes and timely chain inspection.

items.question: Common failure — "What is the most common M270/M271 failure?"
items.answer: For the M271, timing‑chain stretch and tensioner failure are the most critical issues — often presenting as a rattle on cold start, typically between 60,000–100,000 miles. The M270 is much less prone to this but can suffer from camshaft adjuster wear on higher‑mileage examples.

items.question: Fitment — "Which Mercedes-Benz models use the M270/M271?"
items.answer: The M271 powers many early‑2000s C‑Class, E‑Class, CLK and SLK models with 'Kompressor' badges; the later M270 appears in W204 C‑Class, W176 A‑Class, and W246 B‑Class, from around 2011 onward.

items.question: Longevity — "How long does the M270/M271 last?"
items.answer: With regular servicing and preventative chain work on the M271, these engines can exceed 120,000 miles. M270 units are more durable and regularly reach 150,000+ miles when maintained correctly.
